title: Intimate Revolts
summary: The book under review repositions love not as sentiment but as structural critique, exposing how caste infiltrates the most private chambers of the heart.
body_text: A + | A | A - Reader Mode Intimate Revolts Book Reviews Vol. 61, Issue No. 14, 04 Apr, 2026 Kiran Das Caste, Gender, and the Politics of Loving Otherwise Love in the Time of Caste: A Dalit-feminist Anthology of Love Stories edited and translated Nikhil Pandhi, New Delhi: Zubaan Books, 2026; pp 336, ₹ 490. The book under review repositions love not as sentiment but as structural critique, exposing how caste infiltrates the most private chambers of the heart. Love in the Time of Caste: A Dalit-feminist Anthology of Love Stories, edited and translated from Hindi by Nikhil Pandhi, is a crucial intervention in the affective politics of caste. It argues that the anthology repositions love not as private sentiment but as structural critique, exposing how caste regulates intimacy, sexuality, marriage, and reproduction. Across narratives of sexual violence, urban meritocracy, intra-Dalit tensions, reproductive labour, disability and inter-caste desire, the collection demonstrates that caste persists as an affective regime embedded within everyday relationships. title: Putting Studies on Dalit History to Work?
summary: Some years ago, in a review article published in the Modern Asian
body_text: A + | A | A - Reader Mode Putting Studies on Dalit History to Work? Book Reviews Vol. 61, Issue No. 13, 28 Mar, 2026 Sasheej Hegde Identity, Conflict, and Counter-narratives: Dalit Experiences in Culture, Politics, and Stigmatisation edited by Chinna Yagati Rao and Raj Sekhar Basu, Hyderabad: Orient BlackSwan, 2025; pp xv + 342, ₹ 1,160. Some years ago, in a review article published in the Modern Asian Studies , the historian, Chinnaiah Jangam, offered three passing observations about studies on Dalit history, not all of which have received sufficient attention. He argued that (i) Dalit scholarship, as it consolidates itself, must pay “special attention” to the fact that “Dalit identity is not a foolproof construct but one fraught with complexities and tensions”; (ii) nuancing the Dalit category and capturing the heterogeneity of their struggles and articulations requires a “more stringent analysis” of the “intersection of the categories of caste and gender”; and (iii) the historical narra tive of Dalit articulations across India must include “the voices of Dalit leaders and organizers who were Gandhi’s fol lowers” (Jangam 2016: 413–14). I want to return to the underlying thrust of these ideas as they set in place what is distinctive about the work that I am here reviewing for the readers of EPW , while also adding another dimension to our thinking about Dalit histories in the present. title: OBCs, Adivasis, and the Persian Cosmopolis in India’s Heartland
summary: Hindu nationalist forces have attempted to create a sharp separation of communities by religion and caste, pre
body_text: A + | A | A - Reader Mode OBCs, Adivasis, and the Persian Cosmopolis in India’s Heartland Special Articles Vol. 61, Issue No. 12, 21 Mar, 2026 Dolly Daftary The project of majoritarian boundary-making is unsettled when we explore lives lived at the geographic, environmental, and economic margins of India. Islamicate nominative practices among cultivator caste Kolis (denoted Other Backward Classes) and Adivasis, shared spiritual belongings of Kolis, Dalits, and Muslims, and everyday linguistic practices related to governance and justice at the borderlands in Gujarat, illuminate the rich entanglement of semi-subsistence communities with what is called the Persian cosmopolis. Oppositional potentialities to exclusionary identities always remain co-present as traces, foreclosing the erasure of certain pasts and presents, and containing possibilities for the future. Hi ndu nationalist forces have attempted to create a sha rp s eparation of communities by religion and caste, pre scribing and proscribing specific cultural practices for various groups (Nath 2023; Tomar 2022), such as seeking to lay down who to marry ( Indian Express 2023), what to eat (Eva 2022), how to celebrate festivals ( New Indian Express 2023), and what language and idioms to use in communicative practices ( Indian Express 2019). These attempts seek to bring about the severance of ties between social groups. However, commonsensical understanding of these boundaries, as well as this boundary-making project itself, is ruptured when we explore the minutiae of lives lived at the environmental, economic, and social margins. title: Urban Dalit Mobilities
summary: There are claims made in certain academic writings and popular conversations in India that with more economic development, liberalisation, and increased urbanisation, caste discrimination and biases will die down
body_text: A + | A | A - Reader Mode Urban Dalit Mobilities Book Reviews Vol. 61, Issue No. 1, 03 Jan, 2026 Dhiren Swain https://doi.org/10.71279/epw.v61i01.46934 Caste and Emancipatory Quest: Ethnography of Dalits in an Urban Neighborhood by Rama Devi, Palgrave Macmillan, 2025; pp 291, € 39.99. There are claims made in certain academic writings and popular conversations in India that with more economic development, liberalisation, and increased urbanisation, caste discrimination and biases will die down. Rama Devi’s book takes up this issue and investigates the claims through an intersectionality of caste-class and gender approach. In this book, we find critical discussions on the theories of modernisation, neo-liberalism, and urban development, as well as related claims of civility, caste lessness, economic mobility and the fu tures of caste. The book explores the relationship between space and identity in the background of an urbanising and modernising Delhi, specifically fo cusing on a Dalit neighbourhood of Asha Nagar. title: Gender and Caste
summary: I’m a freedom seeker — Kalyani Thakur Charal (Mitra and Sarangi, p 11)
body_text: A + | A | A - Reader Mode Gender and Caste Book Reviews Vol. 61, Issue No. 1, 03 Jan, 2026 Anandita Pan The Bengal Chapter https://doi.org/10.71279/epw.v61i01.46412 Poems of Chandalini: Poems by a Bengali Dalit Womanist, Kalyani Thakur Charal edited and translated by Zinia Mitra and Jaydeep Sarangi, Bolpur: Birutjatio Sahitya Sammiloni, 2023; pp 88, ₹ 275. The perspective on caste in Bengal has seen a drastic transformation t hrough contemporary Dalit writings. Th e Bengali educated and left-aligned community has long claimed to be unmarked by caste. Dalit writings in the Bengali language have come to challenge this proclamation by high lighting the pervasiveness of casteism in Bengal. title: Towards a Humanistic and Inclusive Ecological Perspective
summary: Dalit Ecologies: Caste and Environmental Justice is one of several books by Mukul Sharma that raise critical questions on environmental issues in India.
body_text: A + | A | A - Reader Mode Towards a Humanistic and Inclusive Ecological Perspective Book Reviews Vol. 60, Issue No. 51, 20 Dec, 2025 Ambika Aiyadurai https://doi.org/10.71279/epw.v60i51.45479 Dalit Ecologies: Caste and Environmental Justice by Mukul Sharma , Cambridge University Press, 2024; pp 300, $120. Dalit Ecologies: Caste and Environmental Justice is one of several books by Mukul Sharma that raise critical questions on environmental issues in India. His earlier book, published in 2017, Caste and Nature: Dalits and Indian Environmental Politics, was a seminal book that examined the linkages between caste and nature. That book has become a key text in the course on environmental issues that I teach at IIT Gandhinagar. Building on his earlier works, Sharma expands and builds upon the various Dalit conceptions of caste in his new book, Dalit Ecologies: Caste and Environmental Justice. Though the struggles of Dalits are about land, displacement, and water, there has not been a serious analysis to connect the dots between caste, nature, and environmental justice, which the book addresses. title: Dalit Entrepreneurship in Public Policy
summary: Lack of capital and constrained access to credit have long been recognised as major obstacles in the growth and expansion of micro- and small-sized firms. While credit related challenges are faced by petty entrepreneurs across all castes, but they assume larger magnitude in the case of marginalised social groups.
body_text: A + | A | A - Reader Mode Dalit Entrepreneurship in Public Policy Special Articles Vol. 60, Issue No. 51, 20 Dec, 2025 Akhil Alha K B Saxena What Does the Evidence Suggest? https://doi.org/10.71279/epw.v60i51.44766 Key public policy schemes, such as Micro Units Development and Refinance Agency, Stand-Up India, the Venture Capital Fund for Scheduled Castes, and the 4% Public Procurement Policy, have attempted to foster Dalit and Adivasi entrepreneurship. However, empirical evidence reveals that SC and Scheduled Tribe entrepreneurs remain severely underrepresented in high-value credit categories and government procurement contracts. Neoclassical and new institutional economic frameworks that dominate policy thinking have failed to account for structural caste-based exclusion, which persists within formal credit institutions, undermining the efficacy of these schemes. Lived experiences illustrate how legal property rights and merit are often insufficient to secure institutional credit due to entrenched discrimination. The authors are thankful to the ICSSR for funding the study “Scheduled Caste Entrepreneurship in India: Status, Challenges and Constraints” from which this paper has been extracted. Lack of capital and constrained access to credit have long been recognised as major obstacles in the growth and expansion of micro- and small-sized firms. While credit related challenges are faced by petty entrepreneurs across all castes, but they assume larger magnitude in the case of marginalised social groups. The 2013 Economic Census data—the latest year for which this data is available—shows that only 10% of the units owned by Scheduled Castes ( SC ) and 8.7% of Scheduled Tribe ( ST )-owned units have received financial assistance from government sources. In contrast, Other Backward Classes ( OBC ) account for 32%, while “others” account for 50% of such enterprises. Majority of units owned by SC s are own account enterprises ( OAE s); with 89% of units in rural areas and 72% in urban areas; while their share in the ownership of directory enterprises is only 6%. ST s own only 2.7% of directory enterprise units.1 Only 8.3% of Dalit-owned units and 3.5% of Adivasi-owned units had at least one hired labour in 2013, while this share was 54% for others and 34% for OBC s in the same year. These facts indicate a poor state of entrepreneurship among SC s and ST s. Dear Reader, To continue reading, become a subscriber. Explore our attractive subscription offers. title: Caste, Water, and the Political Ecology of Drought
summary: Drought in India is commonly understood through meteorological classifications or agricultural losses, as reflected in official declarations (Government of Tamil Nadu 2017).
body_text: A + | A | A - Reader Mode Caste, Water, and the Political Ecology of Drought Review of Rural Affairs Vol. 60, Issue No. 49, 06 Dec, 2025 Asifa Zunaidha Feroz Struggles for Equitable Development in Tamil Nadu https://doi.org/10.71279/epw.v60i49.46590 The political ecology of drought and marginalisation in Tamil Nadu’s arid landscapes is examined with a focus on Ramanathapuram district. Drought in this region is not merely a climatic phenomenon but a sociopolitical process shaped by caste hierarchies, landholding inequalities, and historical patterns of unequal resource control. Drawing on qualitative field research, the paper shows how dominant caste groups continue to monopolise access to water and state-relief programmes, while marginalised communities—particularly Dalits and landless groups—face disproportionate vulnerability. A political ecology lens reveals how addressing drought necessitates confronting caste and governance failures in rural India. Dr ought in India is commonly understood through me teorological classifications or agricultural losses, as reflected in official declarations (Government of Tamil Nadu 2017). However, in arid districts like Ramanathapuram in Tamil Nadu, it is a lived and recurring condition. Here, drought does not manifest only as rainfall deficit, but as a daily struggle over water, deepened by caste hierarchies, gendered burdens, and uneven state responses. This paper argues that drought in such contexts is not merely an environmental or technical challenge, but a political phenomenon shaped by historically embedded structures of exclusion. title: Entitlement versus Exploitation
summary: In colonial India, the rise of caste consciousness led many lower castes to reconstruct their histories to reclaim their past glory.
body_text: A + | A | A - Reader Mode Entitlement versus Exploitation Special Articles Vol. 60, Issue No. 48, 29 Nov, 2025 Dhananjay Singh A Critical Examination of Bhikhari Thakur’s Nai Bahar https://doi.org/10.71279/epw.v60i48.43751 Nai Bahar (1932), a Bhojpuri composition by Bhikhari Thakur (1887–1971), is critically examined through the discourse of hakdari (entitlement) and hakmari (exploitation). Unlike caste histories aligned with Sanskritisation, Nai Bahar serves as a lived autobiography of the Nai (barber) caste, exposing systemic oppression within the varna order. Thakur critiques the jajmani system that exploits barbers despite their ritual importance and proposes migration as a means of liberation, dignity, and economic survival. By linking local caste struggles with broader labour migrations, including the girmitiya experience, the text redefines migration as resistance and self-assertion. As the first caste-based autobiographical narrative in Bhojpuri literature, Nai Bahar offers a powerful intervention in Dalit–backward caste histories and labour discourse. The author is indebted to Sadan Jha for his thoughtful suggestions and encouragement. The author also gratefully acknowledges the anonymous reviewers for their comments and critical feedback, which significantly enhanced the clarity and overall quality of the paper. In colonial India, the rise of caste consciousness led many lower castes to reconstruct their histories to reclaim their past glory. Historian Badri Narayan (2006), in his edited book Upekshit Samudayon Ka Atma Itihas , identified this trend in historical writing and categorised it into three distinct phases. In the first phase (1891–1960), historical texts tended towards Sanskritisation, linking lower-caste histories to the lineage of sages and heroes from grand epics, thereby aligning them with upper-caste Brahminical cultural values. The second phase (1960–84) retained this Sanskritising approach but also incorporated “folk history” drawn from oral traditions, further strengthening caste identity. During this period, tensions between Brahminical and non-Brahminical identities became more pronounced. The third phase (post 1984) shifted its focus to asserting Dalit pride, emphasising their sacrifices in nation-building and highlighting historical marginalisation due to upper-caste dominance. This period also saw a strong push for caste-based reservations, demands for special provisions from the Indian state, recognition of their contributions to national development, and greater political representation. (Narayan 2006: 16). Dear Reader, To continue reading, become a subscriber. Explore our attractive subscription offers. Click here Or To gain instant access to this article (download). title: Dalit Politics in Transition
summary: Maya, Modi, Azad: Dalit Politics in the Time of Hindutva provides a nuanced historical and field-based account of the trajectory of Dalit politics in contemporary North India
body_text: A + | A | A - Reader Mode Dalit Politics in Transition Book Reviews Vol. 60, Issue No. 47, 22 Nov, 2025 Sazid Ali https://doi.org/10.71279/epw.v60i47.46388 Maya, Modi, Azad: Dalit Politics in the Time of Hindutva by Sudha Pai and Sajjan Kumar, HarperCollins Publishers India, 2023; pp 336, ₹ 599. Maya, Modi, Azad: Dalit Politics in the Time of Hindutva provides a nuanced historical and field-based account of the trajectory of Dalit politics in contemporary North India , making it a significant contribution to the expanding scholarship on the Dalit political discourse. It traces three “rapidly shifting scenarios in Dalit politics,” each discussed in three sections. First, the capturing of power by the Bahujan Sa maj Party (BSP) through experiments in rain bow coalition politics ( sarvajan ) and its eventual decline in Uttar Pradesh (UP). Second, and perhaps the most interest ing analysis, concerns the Bharatiya Janata Party’s (BJP) success in drawing substantial sections of the Dalit community into its fold un der the garb of “subaltern Hindutva” under Prime Minister Naren dra Modi. The third section highlights the rise of newer and more radical Dalit or ganisations, assessing both the possibilities and the fragmentation they introduce into Dalit politics.
